To determine how different amino acid sequences form similar protein structures, and how proteins adapt to mutations that change t,he volume of residues buried in their close-packed interiors, we have analysed and compared the atomic structures of nine different globins. The homology of the sequences in the two most distantly related molecules is only 16%. The principal determinants of three-dimensional of these proteins are the approximately 59 residues involved in helix to helix and helix to haem packings. Half of these residues are buried within the molecules. The observed variations in the sequence keep the side-chains of buried residues non-polar, but do not maintain their size : the mean variation of the volume among homologous amino acids is 56 AS. Changes in the volumes of buried residues are accompanied by changes in the geometry of the helix packings. The relative positions and orientations of homologous pairs of helices in the globins differ by rigid body shifts of up to 7 b and 30”. In order to retain functional activity these shifts are coupled so that the geometry of the residues forming the haem pocket is very similar in all the globins. We discuss the implications of these results for the mechanism of protein

have remained the same. How can amino acid sequences that are very different form proteins that are very similar in three-dimensional structure? What, is the mechanism by which proteins adapt to mutations in the course of their evolution? These closely related questions present themselves in a particularly concrete form in those families of proteins for which structures are known at atomic resolution, such as t,he globins (Perutz et al., 1965; Love et al., 1971), cytochromes c (dlmassy & Dickerson, 1978), serine proteases (Hartley, 1974). and NAD-binding domains of the dehvdrogenasrs (Rossman et al, 1975). One constraint with which the processes of evolution must contend in accommodating mutations is the close-packed nature of protein interiors. In a, simple “oildrop” model of the interior of a protein the conservation of hydrophobicitp of buried residues would be sufficient to maintain the structure. But the necessity to maintain close packing as well should further limit the types of side-chain substitutions that can occur. Therefore, it seems to us, a central problem in the evolubion of proteins is to understand how changes can occur in the sizes of buried residues? without perturbing the overall molecular structure beyond the limit of viability. To study this question we have examined the relationship between sequence, structure and packing in nine globins. Globins occur in a wide variety of organisms with the common function of oxygen storage and transport. All globins contain the same prosthetic group, the haem, with an iron atom that can reversibly bind molecular oxygen. The beginning of the comparative study of the three-dimensional structures of the globins must’ be assigned to the work of Reichert & Brown (1909), who investigated the evolutionary variation in axial ratios of crystals of haemoglobins isolated from different species. But the true study of the relationship between sequence and structure necessarily awaited the successful application of X-ray crystallography t,o proteins (Zuckerkandl & Pauling, 1965). Crystal structure analyses have provided structures, at atomic resolution, for 11 globins. These are the alpha and beta chains of human and horse haemoglobin. the gamma subunit of human foetal haemoglobin, sperm whale and seal myoglobins, the monomeric haemoglobins of the sea lamprey and an annelid worm (Glycera dibranchata), a larval insect eryt,hrocruorin (Chironomus thummi), and leghaemoglobin from the root nodules of the yellow lupin (see Table I)+. (In addition, a low-resolut,ion hacina has been reported of metmyoglobin from the mollusc Aplysia (Ungaretti et al., 1978).) The perspective provided by this range of species has been essential to our work. These 11 molecules or subunits have very different amino acid sequences but remarkably similar secondary and tertiary structures (see Fig. 1). Eight helices, labelled A, B. C, D: E, F, G and H, are common to all except that four structures lack the small D helix. The helices assemble in a common pattern, enclosing the haem group in pockets of similar geometry made up from homologous portions of the molecules. t Abbreviations of names of molecules: alpha and beta chains of human deoxyhaemoglobin, HHba and HHbfl; alpha and beta strands of horse methaemoglobin, EHba and EHb/T; sperm whale myoglobin, SWMb ; the haemoglobins of sea lamprey, LHb; and GZycera dibranchata, GHb ; erythrocruorin from Chimnomw thummi, CEr; and lupin leghaemoglobin, LgHb.

Perutz et al. (1965) compared the three globin structures and the 18 globin sequences that were then known. Observing from the structures that about 33 residues formed the interior of the molecule, they showed that the residues at 30 of these interior positions have non-polar side-chains in all 18 sequences. They showed also that this restjriction did not apply to residues on the surface, where mutations have frequently replaced polar by non-polar side-chains or vice versa. Later, Ptitsyn (1974) compared 67 globin primary sequences and found that residues at 21 (or 27 if we count Ser and Thr as effectively non-polar) of Perutz’s 33 interior positions are non-polar in all 67 of these sequences. From an examination of 52 sequences, Lim & Ptitsyn (1970) identified 31 posit,ions occupied only by non-polar residues, and which form a common hydrophobic nucleus of the structure. Of these positions, 27 appear in the list of 33 interior positions given by Perutz et al. Lim & Ptitsyn also showed that the total volume of the 31 residues in each sequence in these “core” positions remains almost constant. Kozitsyn & Ptitsyn (1974) calculated the intramolecular contacts between the hydrophobic residues in EHba, EHb/3, SWMb and CEr. In each structure the 27 residues in the hydrophobic core form among themselves about 30 residue to residue contacts. Of these contacts, 15 are common to all five structures. From a further examination of globin sequences, Ptitsyn (1974) described an invariant arrangement of “chain bending” residues.

Huber et al. (1971) compared the three-dimensional structures of Chironomus

erythrocruorin and sperm whale myoglobin. They found that although the individual helices were superposable with a mean difference in atomic position of about O-5 A, the A, C, D, E, F and G helices taken together could not be superposed to a mean displacement of better than 1.25 A. This implies that these helices have different relative positions and/or orientations in the two structures. Huber et al. attributed these shifts principally to deletions in the Chironomus sequence. To identify the microscopic response to mutation and the origin of the common folding pattern, we have compared the sequences and three-dimensional structures of nine globins from different species. Applying computer graphics and numerical superposition techniques to the atomic co-ordinates, we define the similarity of gross structure in quantitative terms, and analyse in detail the packing of the helices and haem group in the nine molecules. Our principal new results are: (1) The principal determinants of the three-dimensional structure of the globins are the approximately 59 residues that are involved in the packing of helices and in the interactions between the helices and the haem group. Only half these residues are buried within the molecules. (2) Although mutations of the buried residues keep the side-chains non-polar, they vary both in amino-acid identity, (there is an homology of only 19% between some distantly related globins) and size, (the mean change in side-chain volume at any position is 56 A3). (3) With the exception of some residues near the termini, most of the helices in globins have geometries close to that of a standard alpha-helix, and are therefore similar to each other. In contrast, the interhelical, or turn, regions have little homology of sequence or structure. (4) In the nine globins, the total volume of the residues that form the interfaces between homologous helices varies by up to 57 yo, (5) Homologous pairs of packed helices cannot be superposed to as good a tit as individual helices: the shifts in relative position and orientation may be as much as 7 A and 30”. (Because helix interfaces are close packed (Richmond & Richmonds, 1978), (5) is a necessary consequence of (3) and (4).) (6) There are five helix packings that occur, with extensive interfacial contact, in all globins studied; these involve the following pairs of helices: A/H, B/E, B/G, F/H and G/H. We describe the structures of the interfaces using the “ridges into grooves” model for helix packing (Chothia et al., 1977). This permits us to classify helix packings in terms of topographic features of the helix surfaces and the way in which they fit together. We find that evolution has preserved the class of packing; that is, the pattern of contacts between residues at homologous helix interfaces is conserved. For the B/G and F/H packings there is little or no conservation of the volume of individual residues at these interfaces in the nine globins. In the nine A/H interfaces there is approximate conservation of the size of two central residues. In the nine B/E packings there is conservation of the relative size of four central residues. The centre of each of the nine G/H packings is formed by one of two different sets of residues.

The common struct,ural features of bhe globins must, arise from interactions between residues that are present in all sequences. Although the nine sequences vary in length between 136 and 154 amino acids, there are, taking into account insertions and deletions, a total of 171 possible residue positions (Table 2). Tn only 116 of these posit,ions do all nine molecules actually contain amino acids. It is in the interactions of amino acids at these 116 common positions that we must seek the factors that determine the common features of the secondary and tertiary structure. The 116 common positionsj- are: 11 to 26,35 to 55,57,59,69 to 89,97 to 113,117 to 136 and 146 to 164. Except for positions 57 and 59 (in the CD corner) they form six consecutive stretches of at least 16 amino acids each, including residues from each of the helical regions except D. On the basis of secondary structure, the common positions may be assigned to three groups : (i) 97 positions

These are 11, 55 and 117, adjacent to the amino- or carboxyl-terminus of a helical region, a’nd the CD corner residues 57 and 59. (iii) 14 positions correspond to alpha-helical regions in some but not all of the molecules: 54,89,97 to 98,111 to 113,118,135 to 136,146 and 162 to 164. The variability of their conformation is in some cases the effect of deletions, insertions or atomic shifts in neighbouring residues, or possibly of small differences in the interpretation of electron density maps. Because most of the residues in the common positions are in helical regions, it is the interactions between the helices, and between them and the haem, that must. be principally responsible for the common tertiary structures of the globins. In order to analyse the variations in amino acid sequences and to trace the structural consequences of this variation, we classify the 116 common positions according to their roles in the tertiary structure. Two categories have proved useful : (1) Helix contact versus non-contact residues. We call all the residues in the 59 positions that form the same helix to helix or helix to haem contact in seve71or more of the nine globins the helix contact residues. We call residues in the other 57 positions non-contact residues. (2) Residues on the surface (exposed to solvent) versus buried residues. Residues in 34 positions have an average accessible surface area of 10 Akz or less (Table 2). We call these buried residues; the others are surfucc: residues. Of the 34 “buried” positions, 31 also contain helix-contact residues (Table 2). We call all the residues in these 31 positions buried helix contact residues (labelled B in Table 2). Of the other three buried positions, 117 contains residues in the FG turn that are in contact with the haem groups: 44 is mostly buried by the BC turn, and 26 in the AB burn. Conversely, there are 28 helix-contact positions whose residues are not buried. We call these the surface-helix contact residues (labelled S in Table 2). In summary, each of the nine globin sequences contains 116 amino acids in positions of common homology. Of these, 97 positions contain residues that are in helices in each globin. Residues at 59 of the 116 common positions form the same interhelix or helix to haem contacts, with few exceptions. Residues at the other 57 positions form no intramolecular contacts, with few exceptions. Of the 59 helix-contact positions, 31 are buried and 28 are on the surface (Table 3). Only five positions out of the 116 common ones have t,he same residue in all nine globins: 11 (Leu), 48 (Pro), 54 (Phe). 57 (Phe), and 109 (His). Two of these, 54 (Phe) and 109 (His), are involved in the binding of the haem to the protein. So mutat,ion at these two positions has yet been identified in any globin sequence. The other positions very commonly have the residues found here but a few exceptions are known (Dayhoff, 1976). The Phe residue at position 57 is in the CD turn and fills a large cavity adjacent to the C helix. The Pro residue at position 48 is in the BC corner. Although there may be a structural rationale for the conservation of hhe Phe residues at positions 54 and 57 and His at position 109, there is no obvious reason for the persistence of Leu at position 11 and Pro at 48. The leucine residues at posit,ion 11 lie on the surfaces of the molecules, the side-chain pointing into a surface cavit’y. We note that nearly all globins contain the dipeptide Leu-Ser or Leu-Thr close to the N-terminus. It is possible that this is a recognition position used in the biosynthesis of the polypeptides or of the corresponding messenger RNAs. The nine prolines at position 48 are surface residues. Their conformations in the nine structures (4 e --BOO, # g -5”) are not special to prolines. Possibly this proline facilitates the folding of globins by inhibiting the C helix from forming a simple continuation of the B helix. The homology of the amino acids in the 116 positions common to all nine globins varies between 16 and 88:; identity of side-chains (see Table 4). Table 4 lists the homology of residues in each class, as well as the total homology of the common positions in each pair of molecules. For closely related globins, the homology of the buried and surface contact residues is always greater than that of the non-contact residues. However, for distantly related globins the buried contact residues have in some cases a lozcer homology than the non-contact residues. This occurs in the comparisons of CEr with HHbcc. In the previous section we described the small degree of preferential conservation of amino acid identity in the 31 buried helix contact positions. Here we confirm that, as previously noted by Perutz et al. (1965) and Ptitsyn (1974) using different data, there is a striking preferential conservation of the hydrophobic character of the amino acids at the buried positions. The 31 buried helix contact positions in our nine globins contain a total of 9 x 31= 279 residues. This set has the following composition: Leu 62 (instances), Val 49, Phe 33, Ala 31, tie 23, Gly 20, His 18, Thr 12, Ser 11. Met, 8: Tyr 5: Cyh 3, Trp 3, Pro 1. Of the 18 His residues, nine are “proximal” and eight are “distal” to the haem iron; and one is His34 in GHb (general position 43). Ser and Thr. although containing a hydroxyl group, act effectively as hydrophobic by forming hydrogen bonds to the backbone (Kendrew, 1962). ISote that there is no particular selection for large hydrophobic side-chains: the list contains Phe 33 times and Ala 31 times. Thus the evolutionary divergence of these nine globins has been constrained by an almost absolute conservation of the hydrophobicity of the residues buried in the helix to helix and helix to haem contacts. Il;o such conservation of chemical type occurs in the other groups of residues. The 28 positions that contain the surface helix to helix or haem contact posit’ions have 28 x 9=252 amino acids: Lys 34 (instances), Leu 30. Ala 27, Val 19, Arg 16, His 13, Ser 13. Gly 12, Glu 11, Phe 11, Asp 10, Tyr 10: Asn 8, Thr 8, Trp 8, Gln 7, Pro 6, Ile 5; Met 3 and Cyh 1. Although most of these residues are non-polar, a residue from the hydrophilic group Arg, Lys, Asp, Glu, Asn, Gin and His occurs at 24 of the 27 positions in at least one globin. Only three positions do not. contain any hydrophilic amino acids: 23, 105 and 130, and the residues at, these positions have a,n accessible surface area: 15 to 17 AZ, that is only a little greater than that’ of the buried residues, < 10 W”. The residues that occupy the 57 non-contact positions are similar to those that occup,v the surface contact positions in their composition and distribution of hydrophilic residues.

(d) The homology qf residue volumes Because the interiors of proteins are close-packed, the v-olumes and shapes of the individual amino acids must play a major role in determining the form of the folded polypeptide chain. In this section we describe the variations in the volumes of individual amino acids found in homologous positions in our nine globins, and of the total volumes of interacting sets of amino acids. The histogram in Figure 2 shows the variation in the volume of homologous residues in the 31 buried helix contact positions. Only four positions contain side-chains with the same volume in all nine globins : 54,75, 109 and 160. The residues at three of these four positions are involved in cont’acts between the haem and protein : 109 is the ironlinked proximal His, 75 is the distal His (Leu in GHb), and 54 is a Phe that packs against the haem group. The residues at the fourth position, 160, (Leu or Ile) are in helix H; they pack against both the F and G helices and. in five of the globins, against, the haem as well. 4. The Homology of the Secondary Structures and Their Spatial Arrangement (a) Definition

and homology of the helical regions

The secondary structures of the globins have the form of a set of helical regions, contiguous in some cases but separated by turns in others. There is no unambiguous way to decide exactly where helices begin and end, because the ends of helices are often distorted from ideal helix geometry. We based our assignments on the original descriptions of the structures, on the geometry of the hydrogen bonding between peptides, and on examination of the molecular conformations, facilitated by computergenerated models. We regard any amino acid t,o be part of a helix if it participates reasonably well in the hydrogen-bonding pattern of the helix, either as donor or acceptor. When in doubt we tended to include borderline cases as helical. There are two major deviations from the globin paradigm of eight helices, originally described in sperm whale myoglobin. (a) The D helix, the shortest and least perfect of the helices, is not present in HHba, EHbu, GHb and LgHb; and its hydrogen bonds are highly distorted in SWMb and LHb. (b) Examination of the co-ordinates of the high-resolution structures reveals an additional stretch of helix in the EF region of mammalian globins. This forms a new helical region, five residues in length and immediately preceding the F helix, which we call the F’-helix. It is homologous with the beginning of the F helices in LHb, GHb, CEr and LgHb (see Table 2). In the mammalian globins the axis of the F’ helix makes an angle of about 135” with that of the F helix (see Fig. 3). To investigate and compare the distortions of the helices from ideal alpha-helix geometry, we have computed least-squares fits of the backbone atomic co-ordinates of the globin helices to the regular polyalanine determined from libre diffraction (Arnott & Dover, 1967), and also superposed them with each other. The root-mean-square deviations of the optimal superpositions are rather large: in the range O-6 to 1.1 A. But except for the C helices, the deviations occur principally in a few residues at the ends of the helices. The inner regions of the globin helices are usually geometrically close in structure to the standard alpha-helix. Superposition of the central regions of the helices with polyalanine gave root-mean-square deviations of 0.2 to 0.5 A. The C helices are exceptions, in that in HHba, HHb/3, EHba, EHbp, SWMb, CEr and LgHb they are 3,, helices. In GHb and LHb the C helices of the currently available co-ordinate sets do not have a simple alpha or 3,a helix geometry.

We conclude that, except for the C regions and for those distorted by internal Pro residues, the helices are close to ideal alpha-helices. The only systematic differences observed are the tendency for carbonyl groups to tilt away from the helix axis (Ladner et al., 1977 ; Steigemann & Weber, 1979), and for some small regions to deform towards a 3,, helix geometry. Distortions at both the amino- and carboxyl-termini of the helices are common. Helices can wind up more tightly, into a 3,, helix ; or unravel, either to a r-helix or simply to a slightly frayed conformation, in which the hydrogen bond of the aminoterminal or carboxyl-terminal residue(s) of the helix is (are) abnormally long or nonlinear. About half the distorted regions involve only the first or last two residues, but distortions can involve one to four residues. Some helices in the globins are bent through angles small enough to be regarded as a single, distorted helix rather than as two contiguous ones. In the A helix of LHb and the G helix of GHb, internal Pro residues cause the helices to bend. In several globins the E helix is bent at position 79 (Ladner et al., 1977; Steigemann & Weber, 1979). The bend is greatest in CEr: 25”. Tertiary structural interactions provide a reasonable explanation. In all the globin helices, except E, the packing of one helix to two other helices involves different side.s of the common helix ; but the packing of the E helix to other helices involves different ends of the E helix. The first part, positions 72 to 80, packs against the B helix while the second part, positions 80 to 87, packs against the A and H helices (see Table 2). The bend may be attributable to conflicting requirements of the two packings for preferred orientations of the E helix. (c) The arrangement

of pairs of packed helices

The nine globin structures contain five major instances of extensive and intimate contact between.homologous pairs of helices: A/H, B/E, B/G, F’F/H and G/H; and four minor helix contacts : A/E, E/H, C/G and B/D. Figure 4 shows the variations in the angle between the helix axes and their distance apart for each of the major packings in each of the globins. The values of interaxial distance and angle observed in the nine structures vary, for any particular packing, by as much as 2 to 3 A and 15 to 30”. These large shifts reflect very significant differences in the spatial assembly of the helices in the various globin structures. In section 3(d) and Table 5 we noted that the total side-chain volume of the residues forming homologous interfaces differed widely among the nine globins. In Figure 5 we plot for each helix packing the distance between the helix axes against the total side-chain volumes. Most of the side-chains that form the interfaces are only partially buried by the interhelical contacts; therefore, the addition or loss of atoms can occur at the sides of the interface without affecting the geometry of the packing. However, it is apparent in Figure 15 that for the five major packings the variation in the distance; between the helix axes is dependent on the variation in the total volume of the side-chains that form the interfaces. The type of ridges that occur in interactions between helices determines, to a first approximation, what the interaxial angle will be. If an interface is formed by packing f4n ridges from both helices, the angle between the helix axes will be N -50” (Fig. 7). If one helix presents f4n ridges and the other the AIn ridges, the angle will be N -110”. In some helix-helix contacts a ridge may have a small residue such as Gly or Ala adjacent to a large one such as Leu or Phe. Helices with ridges containing gaps can form packings such that a ridge from a second helix crosses the ridge of the first at the gap. In the nine globin structures, the B/G and F/H helix packings are formed by the intercalation of f4n ridges and grooves, the A/H packings involve fin ridges of A and the +4n ridges of H, and the B/E and G/H packings are “crossed-ridge” structures. In describing the effects of mutations on the structure of the helix-helix contact,s, we shall attribute changes in the overall geometry of a pair of interacting helices to the mutations that occur at the interface itself. This is a good approximation: for example, the binding of a ligand by the beta subunits of human haemoglobin produces a, small (ml A) but significant shift of the E helix relative to the B helix (Baldwin & Chothia, 1979). For the cases we discuss the differences in helix geometry are greater than this and are principally the result of mutations. 6. The Structure and Variations of the Haem Pocket (a) The structural

basis of the haem-protein

interaction

The prosthetic group of the globins is the haem, an iron porphyrin with eight substituents: two propionic acid groups, two vinyl groups and four methyls. The two propionic acid groups distinguish a single “hydrophilic edge” and the asymmetric distribution of vinyl and methyl groups breaks the mirror symmetry that remains (see Fig. 14(b)). The crystal structure determinations suggest that the haem adopts a unique orientation in at least six of the nine globins (Fermi, 1976 ; Ladner et al, 1977 ; Takano, 1977; Phillips, 1978; Steigemann BEWeber, 1979). The polypeptide chain of each globin forms a crevice or pocket into which the haem fits, with just the hydrophilic propionic acid edge exposed to the solvent. The iron atom of the haem forms a covalent bond to the N-E2 of a histidine in the F helix, at position 109. In some structures one or both of the propionic acid groups form hydrogen bonds to a side chain of the polypeptide. The non-polar portion of the haem interacts with the polypeptide by means of packing contacts. The residues that are in contact, with the haem in the nine globins are listed in Table 6. A total of 15 positions contain residues that make contact with the haem group in 7, 8 or 9 of the globins. These residues belong to five regions of the protein: the carboxyl-terminal end of the C helix, the central portion of the E helix, the F helix (including the covalently bonded proximal His at position log), the FG corner and the G helix. Figure 14 shows the spatial arrangement of these regions around the haem. In terms of the orientation of the haem in part (a) of t,his Figure, the F helix and FG corner pack over much of the top of the haem; the G helix packs against the back of the haem in the region of ring 3, the end of the C helix packs on its right against the bottom and side of the haem, and the E helix packs on its left against the bottom and side. Between the back of ring 2 and the B and H helices there is a space. Adjacent to the haem iron there is a cavity that can accommodate ligands. The His residue at position 75 in the E helix (or Leu in GHb) acts as a trap door at the entrance to this cavity (Morimoto et al., 1971). As a result of different relative positions of the F helix, in HHba and p, EHba and 8, and LgHb two residues from the H helix also form contacts with the top of the haem group.

(b) The effect of mutations

on the haem pocket

Mutations in the polypeptide might affect the structure of the haem pocket in two ways. First, mutations in the individual residues in contact with the haem group might directly alter the shape of the molecular surface that accommodates it. Second, mutations at the helix interfaces that change the relative positions and orientations of the helices might indirectly alter the general shape of the haem pocket. However,

because both sides of a helix interface are flexible, with respect both to conformation and evolution, whereas the haem has comparatively little flexibility, we would expect, mutations at the sites of haem contact, residues to be more difficult, to accommodatr. (i) The relative positions

of the peptides forming

the huem pocket

To study the variability in the structures of the peptides that form the haem pocket, we superimposed the porphyrin rings of the haems in each pair of globins, and examined the relative positions of the peptides. The set of ten homologous residues that form the sides and bottom of the haem pocket (those from the end of the C helix, from the E helix and from the G helix) show only small differences in structure. The positions of the homologous a-carbons of nearly all these residues shift by no more than 2 A relative to the haem, and the other few by no more than 3 A. These differences are smaller than many of those found at helix interfaces, and show that the changes in helix packings cannot be independent, but must be coupled to maintain the structure of the haem pocket. Figure 16 illustrates the arrangements of the B, E and G helices around the haem in EHba and LgHb. The two haem groups are superposed. The E and G helices pack against the haem group and helix B. The B helices do not pack against the haem, nor do helices E and G pack aginst each other. The shift in the position of the E helix relative to the B helix, between EHba and LgHb, is 7 A; and the relative shift of the G helix is 4 A (see Fig. 6). The two shifts are coupled so that the residues in contact with the haem differ in position by 2 A or less. The top of the haem pocket is formed by the F helix and FG corner. The positions of the F helix relative to haem differ in the globins, though its axis is kept parallel or nearly parallel to the haem plane. The interactions between the haem and the polypeptide chain occur in the F helix and are most important in that they have direct functional significance. The His residue at position 199 is covalently bonded to the iron at the centre of the haem and differences in the relative positions of the F helix and the haem involve differences in the torsion angle of the side-chain of this His residue and the Fe-N-E2 bond. Insertions and deletions change the structure of the FG corner, and particularly the way in which the side-chain at position 113 makes its contact with the haem. Elsewhere we shall describe these structural differences in detail and relate them to the oxygen affinities of the globins. (ii) Mutations

of the haem contact residues

Like the helix-helix interfaces, the peptides that make contact with the haem group tend to form a surface of complementary shape. Inspection of the peptides that pack against the haem shows that two, those from the E helix and the end of the C helix, are L-shaped with the vertical side packing against the side of the haem and the horizontal side against its bottom face (see Fig. 15). The residues that pack against the face tend to be conserved, while those that pack against the side are more readily mutable, because only the first one or two of their side-chain atoms are involved in the contacts with the haem. Thus, at the end of the C helix, position 54 always contains a Phe residue that packs against the bottom face of the haem group; but the residues at position 53 pack against the right side of the haem and can be LYS, Val, Leu, Phe or Tyr. The Regions Between Successive Helices, or the “Turns” “turn” regions show hardly any subIn contrast to the helices, the intervening stantial conserved fea.tures of conformation or sequence homology. Insertions and deletions are common in turns, but none is found in the interior of a helix. The aligned sequences in Table 2 contain about, 60 positions between helices. Tn each individual globin, residues are present at only one-third of these positions. Indeed, only four of these positions, 55, 57, 59 and 117, are occupied in all nine globins. The structures of the turn regions are different in all but the most closely related

This is the result of a combination of factors : the varying lengths of the turns ; their lack of sequence homology; the differences in the exact lengths of the helices; and differences in the positions and orientations of the ends of the helices, arising, as we have seen in the previous section, from mutations at interfaces between pairs of packed helices. Even in the closely related horse and human haemoglobins, homologous turn regions have conformational differences. The flexibility of the turns is illustrated by the different structures of deoxy and liganded human haemoglobin. The addition of a ligand and the change in quaternary structure result in rigid body shifts of N 1.5 .& by the F helices and N 1 A by the E helices of the beta subunit. These movements are dissipated in the adjacent turn regions (Baldwin & Chothia, 1979). The only join between two helices that has the same structure in all nine globins is that between B and C. This is a special case: not a “turn” in the usual sense, but a single residue, at position 47, that is usually part, of both the B and C helices: In most globins the carbonyl group of the residue at position 47 forms a hydrogen bond with the residue at position 43 in the B helix and its amino group forms a hydrogen bond with the residue at position 50 in the C helix. Helices B and C both pack aginst helix G. The interfaces contain the residues at position 47, and those on either side, at 43 and 50. Any insertion or deletion of residues at this join would radically change the way in which the short C helix is packed aginst the G helix. In the five globins that have the short D helix, and therefore a join between D and E, there is also only a single residue, at position 69, joining the two helices. Both D and E helices pack against the B helix, and residues close to the join, at positions 67 and 72, are involved in the packing. The AB join is much more variable than the CB and DE joins. Helices A and B both pack against the E helix, but in this case the helices are all long and the AB join is not so close to the centre of the packing. Here deletions and insertions are acceptable. In addition to linking the helices into a continuous chain, the residues in turns contribute to stabilizing the native conformation by helping to close off the interior to solvent. Because the globin helices pack with their axes inclined at between 20 and 90” to each other, their ends tend to splay apart. Residues of interhelical regions occupy these spaces in order to keep the gaps close-packed and inaccessible to solvent. Usually such contacts bury only a minor part of the surfaces of the residues of the turns, but in a few cases more is involved. Only Phe is found at position 57. It packs between helices B and C and has an average accessible surface area of 16 AZ. Val or Ile at position 117 packs between the G helix and haem, and has an average accessible surface of 4 A2. In eight of the globins, Phe! Met or Trp at posit,ion 141 packs between the G and H helices with an average accessible surface of 20 A2. In LHb this residue and the beginning of the H helix are deleted.

is structural

change

Mutations that change the size of buried residues change the structure of a protein. For the globins, the structural changes are principally rigid-body shifts of the helices and the dissipation of these movements at the burns, which occur on the surface and vary in length and conformation. This type of structural response to mutation is consistent with the nature of the structural flexibility of globin molecules implied by the close-packed, solid-like core, and less-constrained surface (Frauenfelder et al., 1979). The progress of structural change through a succession of mutations that do not affect function directly is limited by the need to maintain reasonable thermodynamic stability. The free energy of native proteins is only 8 to 15 kcal per mole lower than that of the unfolded state (Privalov & Khechinashvili, 1974). This implies that a mutation can be acceptable only if it costs no more than about 3 to 10 kcal per mole. If an energetically “expensive” mutation does occur, the next mutation must either be complementary (in the sense that it tends to restore the previous structure). or else it must stabilize the st’ructural change that the previous mutation had caused. Although complementary mutations do occur (one example is at the small interface between the E and H helices, which has the contact Phe71-Tyr130 in the /l subunit of human haemoglobin and Leu71-Trp130 in the y subunit of human foetal haemoglobin (Frier & Perutz, 1977) they are not, the rule. The cz priori probability t,hat, a mutation will be both adjacent and complementary to the one that preceded it is too small. (b) The structural

One result of our comparisons of the globin structures is the possibility of describing, in purely structural rat,her than thermodynamic terms, the limits to tolerable perturbations of structure by mutations. The small energy available for the accommodation of a mutation makes it unlikely that a rearrangement of the secondary structure, or an alteration in the pattern of residue contacts at a helix-helix interface, could occur during evolution. Such changes would require the creation of new interfaces that are comparably well-packed, and that buried as much surface as the old, while maintaining the chain connectivity and the geometry of the haem pocket.

Conservation of the general pattern of residue-residue contacts and therefore of the packing class of the helix interfaces limits the variation in relative position and orientation of the two helices. This is why mutations produce only small, local shifts in the structure, rather than large-scale disruption of the pattern of assembly (see also Zuckerkandl & Pauling, 1965). The extent to which mutation can occur at the individual residue positions in a helix interface depends on the packing class. The A/H interface is formed by the k In residue ridges of A packing onto the *4n ridges of H, with conservation of the approximate size of the residues at two sites at the centre of the interface. The B/G and F/H interfaces are formed by the intercalation of two sets of +4n, residue ridges, and here there is little conservation of the size of individual residues. The B/E interface, where the &4n rows cross, conserves the relative sizes of the residues at the central four sites. These different susceptibilities bo mutation arise from the geometrical requirements of different packing classes. The direction of the larger structural changes also depends on the packing class. In response to a mutation at the interface of two helices it is clear that the easiest movement is for the ridge of one to go along the groove of the other. For example, at the B-G interface the grooves, &4n, are inclined N 25” to the helix axis (Fig. 9(c)) and the larger structural changes are approximstely parallel to this direction (see Fig. 6). For helix packings movements of up to 6 to 8 A can take place without significant changes in the pattern of residue contacts (Fig. 17). The necessity to conserve t.he functional properties of an active site places additional, and severe, restrictions on protein evolution. For a mutation at the interface between helices or sheets to be acceptable, it must’ preserve the structural and energetic integrity of that interface, and the rearrangements t,hat it induces must preserve the geometry of the active site (or modify the geometry in a neutral or selectively advantageous \vay). These limitations would be greatest for a protein with an extended binding site, though they might be partially offset if the substrate were flexible. Subunit interfaces, or other interactions imposing constraints on the geometry of the protein surface remote from the active site. will add further restrictions to the acceptability of mutations. Nevertheless, active site geometry does not appear to limit the magnitu,des of possible individual structural shifts. Their magnitude is limited principally by the low free energy of proteins preventing changes in the pattern of residue contacts at helix interfaces. The geometry of the active site is retained by coupling the shifts. Thus functional considerations restrict evolution by determining which of the structurally possible shifts can be accepted and so which of the possible pat’hways of structural change can be explored. The maintenance of non-polar residues at sites of side-chains buried within the globin structure is such a feature: it creates a type of molecule that, by combining tolerance of small deformation with resistance to large disruption of structure, is adapted to the process of evolution itself. For consider the alternative : pairs of polar side-chains hydrogen-bonded and buried in the protein interior would produce two restrictions to mutation. First, there would be a loss of several kcal if a mutation in one of the side-chains deprived the other buried polar group of its hydrogen bond. This would of course be offset if another mutation caused the second side-chain to become non-polar. Second, and more important, the geometrical requirements of hydrogen bonds will limit any helix shift that might occur in a response to the mutation in a residue adjacent to the polar groups. (Ser and Thr have polar side-chains that are found buried within proteins, but these nearly always form hydrogen bonds to the main chain oxygen of a residue in the same helix (Kendrew, 1962), and therefore would not produce the restrictions to mutations just described.) Therefore, the process of evolution will itself assemble non-polar side-chains in the protein interior.

9. Conclusions The three-dimensional structure of the globins is determined principally by the residues that form the interhelical and helix-haem packings. Many combinations of side-chains can produce helix interfaces that are comparably well-packed, and with

[Page 47]

OF THE

geometries which are similar but not iden.tical. Significant restrictions on the size of individual residues are observed only in the rarer types of helix packings, and then only for a few residues at the centre of the interface. The necessity to maintain functional activity makes the differences in hehx geometry produced by mutations interdependent: the shifts are restricted by the cumulative effect on the geometry of t,he haem pocket and the ligand binding site. The globins found in nature are a subset of the range of possible proteins of similar structure. The range of molecules observed is circumscribed by (1) having evolved through a series of low-energy mutations, that produce limited structural changes, and (2) by the functional advantage of any modulation of the ligand affinity that these changes induce.
